original_abstract:
The growth of lithium dendrites in rechargeable lithium-ion and lithium-metal batteries presents a major obstacle to their safety and longevity. Developing effective mitigation strategies requires a deep, fundamental understanding of how these structures evolve under real-world operating conditions. In this study, we utilize in situ $^7$Li magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to simultaneously monitor the electrolyte concentration gradients and the morphological changes of lithium metal electrodes within symmetric cells. 

Our approach relies on chemical shift (CS) imaging, a highly sensitive diagnostic tool that differentiates between various lithium microstructures based on their distinct signatures. We observed that mossy lithium deposits begin to form at the electrode surface almost immediately upon charging in every cell studied. In contrast, true dendritic growth—which spans the gap between electrodes—is triggered only after a specific delay. To quantify these processes, we developed simple metrics to track dendrite initiation and electrolyte depletion across a range of current densities.

The results reveal two distinct growth regimes. At high charge rates, we found a strong correlation between the onset of dendrite growth and the local depletion of lithium salts at the electrode surface, a phenomenon both captured experimentally and predicted by the theoretical Sand’s time model. However, at low current densities, dendrites emerge much earlier than theory suggests, indicating a separate growth mechanism that is not governed by bulk salt depletion. By providing a direct link between solid-state electrode morphology and liquid-phase concentration changes, this MRI-based methodology offers a robust framework for investigating and controlling dendrite formation in next-generation batteries.
humanized_abstract:
Lithium dendrite growth is a widely recognized issue affecting the safety and lifespan of rechargeable lithium-ion (Li-ion) and lithium-metal (Li-metal) batteries. To develop practical dendrite mitigation solutions, there is an urgent need for fundamental knowledge of the battery operation under real conditions. In this work, in situ 7Li magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is employed to provide real-time information on electrolyte concentration distribution and the evolution of lithium metal electrode structure in symmetric Li cells.

By coupling high-resolution ex-situ 7Li chemical shift (CS) imaging with full-scale electrochemical profiling, we gain a quantitative and compositional understanding of the evolution of lithium surfaces in operational lithium ion batteries. Our measurements revealed that mossy lithium forms at the surface of all electrodes very early in almost all cycling experiments regardless of operating conditions. True dendrites bridging the electrodes are found only after a significant delay. Metrics describing the timing of dendrite formation and electrolyte consumption over a range of operating current densities are derived to guide interpretation.

We elucidate the underlying mechanism controlling the lithium electrodeposition process by elucidating the conditions under which both uniform and nonuniform electrodeposition proceed. Under high charge rates, the onset of nonuniform dendrite growth is strongly correlated with lithium salt depletion at the electrode surface, a phenomenon that we are able to quantify both experimentally and theoretically (via application of Sand’s time) for the first time. In contrast, at low current densities the onset of the nonuniform structure is significantly earlier than that predicted theoretically, indicating the presence of a short timescale electrodeposition regime that is not governed by bulk salt depletion. Our MRI technique provides direct and quantitative in situ information on the relationship between the resulting solid electrode structure and the liquid electrolyte solution phase concentration changes throughout the electrodeposition process, enabling detailed understanding and subsequent control of the complex electrochemical phenomenon of lithium dendrite growth and hence providing valuable knowledge for the improvement of new battery technologies.

document.output:
